other_observers: Peter Derven, Durango, CO.; I found the bird. No one else saw the bird independently.
specific_location: The bird was seen along FR 651 on Eight Mile Mesa. The location was 4.5 miles from the intersection of FR 651 and US 84.
description: The sparrow was perched on a branch and facing me. The bird had 2 long, bold white stripes on each side of the head: one a bold supercilium, the other a malar stripe. These white stripes contrasted with the gray auricular patch, and the black color of the chin, throat and chest. From this anterior view of the bird, the black on the chest widened and formed an oval bib which tapered to somewhat of a point at the bottom. Otherwise, the bird was mostly gray in color. The bill was conical. The tail was relatively long and rounded. The length of the bird was similar to a Chipping Sparrow's length, although the build of the bird seemed not quite as thin (proportionally) as a Chipping Sparrow's build. The determination that the bird was a Black-throated Sparrow was made without the need for an earlier step of acknowledging that the bird was a sparrow.Behaviors: I first saw the bird perched in a Gambel's Oak. Soon after giving directions on the location of the bird to my friend, the bird flew into the grass. From that vantage point, the bird was concealed from me.Call: No vocalizations were heard.Plumage: Adult
